_comment: Interpretive English redrafts of Polish notes, keyed by item id. Faithful to meaning, not literal. Test batch of curated favorites — confirm the voice, then scale. Built into notes_en.js by build_notes_en.py.
walkabout: A masterpiece. An improbable, doomed communion between worlds that should never have met — Roeg distils the surreal essence of Australia and finds an aching, wordless beauty in life at the very edge of the world.
ikiru: Every bit the equal of 12 Angry Men — it brings together cinema's very best instincts, and the result stands first-class in both what it says and how it's made.
la-haine: Deftly narrated and directed, with a razor-sharp cross-section of society; it slackens a little midway, but I walked out of the cinema shaken — naturalism in its purest form.
possession: As if The Shining and The Exorcist were fused and redlined — astonishing. Cosmic performances, execution, makeup, and acting. Wow.
burning: How much can be conveyed through such subtle, immaculate craft from the whole ensemble. The second half didn't fully satisfy me, but it's intensely hypnotic cinema.
12-angry-men: Quite literally — the execution of this format is cinema absolute.
oldboy: So much here lands exceptionally — the direction above all, and the balance it strikes between comedy and drama. But the ending wrecked me like nothing has in a very long time.
apocalypse-now: A steady descent into depth and the grotesque, its heavy atmosphere matched by a production as monumental as its subject. Artful, psychologically charged, resonant on many levels — and a superb adaptation.
age-of-success: Fight Club on steroids, in Korean — a staggering cross-section of the chase for success, of consumerism, corporate ruthlessness, the whole capitalist hierarchy.
red-desert: Hyper-atmospheric, achingly beautiful direction, moving in lockstep with the performances and the film's foundation — which keeps unfolding over time, and with such rare subtlety.
human-condition: Flawless cinematography; despite the 3.5 hours you never feel the length. The acting sits very high, and the central theme grips hard within the world it lays out.
network: A masterful dissection of the media, with moments that bring you completely to your knees. Astonishingly written monologues and shattering performances.
the-housemaid: An outstanding piece — it floors you with the quality of its craft, its content, and the execution of the whole format, which barely lets up across the runtime.
woman-under: One of those films that's very good but commits so fully to its thematic course that it all but exhausts it — devastating performances, execution, and character dynamics.
face-of-another: Heavy, with magnificent direction and an intellectual payload that weighs on you hard.
2001-space: Considering it's from 1968… fine, 10/10 — but that finale… maybe the 10/10 is generous :<
short-film-killing: The direction and cinematography are something special, but it's the way it exhausts its subject — a sharp, unflinching vivisection — that lands like a thunderclap.
shame: Thunderous lead performances; a very strong script and production design, and an execution that pins you to your seat, paired with almost pedantically captured detail.
weather-forecast: A Polish One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest.
pygmalion: Surprisingly superb directorial and acting craft, with a story you'd call a flagship classic — beautiful, its many threads raising the stakes.
tar: A masterful marriage of every kind of subtlety, precise as it meanders through all manner of intricacies — but it drifts somewhat nowhere, missing the dot over the i.
corpus-christi: Superb craft, cinematography, light. Very naturally acted and a strongly drawn social portrait; it leaves you with plenty to turn over.
there-will-be-blood: The mood is so thick you could chew through it. The narrative scope impresses, and so does its completeness. Great acting and a certain form of naturalism.
blade-runner-2049: I hadn't seen anything like it in ages — built on an ambrosia of illusion, with a real dose of freshness. Paradoxically it lacked atmosphere, and that ending… More, please!
black-mirror: A future phenomenon on the scale of Breaking Bad…
westworld: Total awe at the writing and execution of this hugely ambitious production — all its detail, subtlety, and multidimensional depth.
green-planet: Outstanding — they outdid Planet Earth II; the best scenography and cinematography I've seen on a screen, and everywhere else they pierced through the zenith remarkably often.
six-feet-under: Opens with a bang and closes with one too; it dips and soars, but as a concept — a blend of the grotesque pulled off in remarkable style — there's something inimitable in it.
soul: Almost wholly filled with subtleties, detail, and beautiful content that surprises and connects; technically and emotionally, it just melts you.
belladonna: The most beautiful and at the same time the saddest animation I've ever seen — the artistry simply has no comparison; it shocks, yet utterly enraptures.
spider-verse: Stylistically, probably the finest feature-length animation I've ever seen — a testament to the genres it represents, and a magnificent spectacle.
antichamber: One of those games that outright changes the way you think — for me, logical genius.
come-and-see: The human portraits captured here are unearthly; the cinematography, though it stumbles, has moments of genius — and the sheer weight of the film reaches far beyond my capacity to digest it.
ernest-celestine: Immaculate animation, a script accented with perfect precision — there's a moral, parallels, symbolism — and all of it accessible and utterly lovely.
kaiba: The quintessence of what art actually is — even as it loses its way, its thematic range, subtleties, and aesthetics reach a synergy like nothing else in a sea of surrealism.
ldr: There's everything in here. One of the best series I've ever seen. Genius.
premier-automne: An absolutely gorgeous, exquisite animation, fusing in its most immaculate formula subtleties utterly unrivalled — and very, very rarely encountered.
tlou2: The concept is so strong it's hard to believe it's packed into a mainstream video-game production — I haven't played anything realized like this in a long time.
mirrors-edge: A surprisingly good, innovative game, marrying the freedom that defines parkour with shooting and a first-person perspective. Short; superb audiovisual presentation.
atlanta: Surprisingly natural, with wonderfully played roles and an intriguing script full of a certain twisted naturalism.
bioshock: The setting is one thing, the atmosphere and deft mechanics another — but this game ignites something you rarely witness in games, and that's what earned it its renown.
dead-set: Superbly written, superbly acted; the direction and camera-judder grate — yet overall a very pleasant surprise, and one of the better zombie watches I've seen.
escape-cinema: Ooh, what narrative mésalliances — the very fabric came alive :)
fleabag: Densely packed with brilliantly arranged, composed material, performed almost flawlessly — very natural to take in, and the writing is masterful.
hellsing: Extreme, exhilarating — terrific work and a true spectacle. Despite its outrageously sharp accents, the series keeps an uncanny balance and holds its level across its long production span.
hilda: Fantastic, colorful, beautiful animation with a sensational soundtrack — full of fluidly realized ideas, watchable in a single breath.
ico: Now that's a journey I can get behind! More an experience than a game.
in-treatment: The show's structure draws its strength from the writing, the cast of characters, and a firm grounding in psychology — even if it's rolled out rather selectively and skin-deep.
eizouken: Really, it's all here — the craft in the details, a ton of imagination, and a dreamer's realm that truly soars, touching gently while reaching far.
konosuba: What. Capital-grade animation dissolved in the squalid fumes of absurdity cranked to the maximum… which amuses to no end and rarely lets off the gas. What cinema! :D
made-in-abyss: A unique cross-genre mix — a fairytale setting laced with cruelty, closer in mood to Might & Magic. More of an 8/10, but the creators don't screw around.
manifold-garden: One of those puzzle games that's gorgeous, inventive, and blows your mind. Maybe I'd hoped for more difficult puzzles, but an incredible experience all the same.
mary-and-max: It starts off sluggish, but the degree of the grotesque and the heaviness run as high as the craft, and it stirs you through its stylistic and intellectual intricacies.
max-payne-2: Mature in its storytelling, with very satisfying mechanics; everything's seemingly improved over the original and still feels fresh — but it was missing that little something extra.
yamadas: A fairly unique animation style, but it was the density and nuances of the writing that won me over — I could identify with most of the situations it portrays.
galactic-railroad: A very peculiar children's film, a Japanese answer to Alice in Wonderland. One of those works the subject and atmosphere of which turn a full 180 degrees.
opm: I love watching a massive satire of the genre it represents — with genuinely interesting content, superb execution, and a breath of fresh air in its own right :)
paranoia-agent: Absolutely outstanding in its own way — the construction of the script, its structure and content are impressive, but the execution cries out for an extra finesse that comes and goes.
pierwsza-milosc: Very complete, capturing a fabulous wealth of detail; ostensibly a documentary, yet it transcends the format, serving up an almost immaculate portrait of a slice of human life.
planet-earth-ii: Absolutely gorgeous — it raises the bar the first one had already set high, weaving in naturally occurring narrative threads while holding to the very highest class of craft.
promare: Just when you think they can't keep this up, it stuns you — the production powers on with an incredible dose of artistic fantasy. Visually beautiful.
redline: An exhilarating production with superb detail, fairly creative illustration, and a really nice structure — you'll watch it flushed with excitement :)
short-circuit: Top-tier animation straight from the Disney stable, with elements that at times are decent, at times push into territory even the best would struggle to reach.
subnautica: An absolutely fantastic exploration experience, with surprisingly deep mechanics, a scaling world, a pinch of story, and solid audiovisual presentation.
the-expanse: A little-known, well-acted cast, a multilayered and expansive plot, direction handled with relative finesse. This is exactly the kind of sci-fi I'd been craving ( :
tlou: Honestly, it's not some giant revelation, but it gets genuinely interesting closer to the end — even if the concept has by now been well-worn by others.
nightmare-christmas: Quite a surprise, just how packed with cultural references this film is — and the execution is buttoned up to the very last detail.
peasants: A visual feast, underpinned by a plot that's strong, solid, and adapted almost fantastically given the dull source material. Surprisingly universal.
kaguya: Truly gorgeous animation, a lovely art style, and an interesting story too — Takahata's films always have something that makes them stand out, even at Ghibli :)
twd-game: Game of the year 2012. Far better than the show. Significantly better than most titles the games industry has put out in recent years.
wolf-among-us: Stylistically and narratively strong — and Telltale's whole approach to games clicks beautifully here.
tokyo-godfathers: What an astonishing, original screenplay — phenomenally written, phenomenally made, considered down to the last full stop. Satoshi Kon's genius in its purest form.
undertale: I love it when a game gets its hooks into you the way Undertale does — and the whole thing is polished down to practically the last button.
urzad: If any film captures what bureaucratic hell actually feels like, it's this one — and it plays out so naturally, right down to that ending!
watchmen: A super-production through and through — phenomenal makeup, a coherent and genuinely fascinating artistic vision; it has its lapses, but the quality is first-rate.
edith-finch: Gorgeous interior design and inventive little stories, some of them landing a real emotional blow. This is the kind of game you want to play!
wic: A game that simply refuses to age. Superb mechanics that hold up just as well in the frantic multiplayer as in a singleplayer carried by a genuinely good narrative thread.
kino-journey: A wonderful format for exploring philosophical and political themes, wrapped in beautiful color — though it loses its footing and frays as it goes, and can turn too blunt.
angels-egg: Surrealism-lite, beautiful to look at; the facial work is stiff, the mood unsettling. You're honestly at a loss for how to score it — and yet it stays a fairly intriguing proposition.
silent-voice: Sub 6/10, dub 8/10. A superb concept overall, but the execution is all over the place — the effort of juggling such a sprawling subject backfires. Still, the dubbed version makes its case.
aot: S1: very bleak stylisation, overcranked heroics and brutal extremes, gorgeous linework — but too much exaggeration and too much character hand-wringing. S2 fell off a cliff, WTF; S3 gets by.
avatar-tla: Cartoons come in all kinds, but this one is something else. For kids and adults alike — very funny, yet built on a beautifully drawn world that makes its heavier threads easy to take in.
gunnm: A more interesting take than the live-action adaptation — a solid spectacle with the more intriguing ideas, and a proper narrative surge when it counts.
berserk: A Might & Magic sort of watch that keeps its level high throughout — and the finale is one of the finest spectacles I've ever come across in anime, full stop.
better-call-saul: It gets better with every season, even if — set beside Breaking Bad — it started out from fairly humble heights.
black-lagoon: Exhilarating, with terrific action; it drags here and there and has its weak spots, but none of it diminishes the whole.
blinded-by-lights: A convincingly built world, let down by badly uneven execution. Plenty of craft missteps — but on the whole the series stands its ground.
laputa: The sheer detail poured into this film is staggering — Animation with a capital A, the gold standard.
chernobyl: There's something very... British about this Chernobyl. That aside, it's solidly made; the trailers promised something more terrifying, and it landed softer than that — but it's good.
colorful: The plot is stretched to its limit, yet held on a tight leash of realism — a watch that's beautiful and painful in its own way, resonating with near-flawless technical precision.
cowboy-bebop: A brilliant mésalliance of everything — though its scattershot structure does, imho, give it a bit of trouble.
fma-brotherhood: Layered, dense with allusions to the modern world, to history and cultures, with superbly escalating action, strong characters, and — most of the time — a terrific script.
ghost-hound: The way it strings its sequences together, the sound design, and the writing can be intriguing and original enough to earn a certain kind of respect.
gits-sac: The series flashes real brilliance in almost every episode — often quite forcefully — even if it's laid out in a fairly conventional format. It got my gears turning all the same :)
gurren-lagann: The way the story keeps scaling up here is phenomenal, and so is the energy, the sound, and the half-serious way it treats everything. At times it truly shines, but it loses steam as it goes.
haibane: Very gentle, with a heavy dose of the metaphorical, balancing the two with grace — even if it veers a touch too far into quasi slice-of-life, though it still holds together firmly.
hinterland: The amateur production does rather jump out at you, but the mix of styles and the sheer poetry of it made a very strong impression on me.
invincible: Sharp and fast-moving — it may be a comic book, but the balance of power still has to add up, and unfortunately they juggle the momentum and the personalities a little loosely.
jin-roh: Animation that blends its subtleties with a grace and resonance few can match — and even when it eases off the gas, the highs are so good and so long that, oh man.
kite: Fairly peculiar and a little naive, but the very natural staging of the fight scenes and the rather raw atmosphere raise the stakes.
life-is-strange: Nice aesthetic, emotional twists — even if some of it's lifted from elsewhere; the game's still a lot of fun for all that.
macross-plus: One of the more impressive finales I've ever seen. It plays a little off the notes I'd have wished for, the style's a touch dated, but the payoff blew me away.
mononoke: Its bite-size vignettes of psychological horror and that unconventional style pull more than their weight — enough to overcome the low-budget production and its other blemishes.
eva: Very peculiar; what captivates most is its superb portrayal of the psychological states of specific characters.
odrzucone: I have a soft spot for humor this plain and unpretentious — though the framing, a handful of moments, and the ending especially leave their mark.
pitbull: Atmospheric, rough, realistic — a superb depiction of post-communist reality from the police's perspective.
psycho-pass: It clicked for me around episode 14 — an interesting, distinctive vision of dystopia with some very strong episodes; you might have hoped for more, but it does catch fire in time.
samurai-champloo: Brilliant humor! An authentic, singular atmosphere, beautifully drawn characters, full-blooded dubbing, an intriguing immersion in hip-hop, superb choreography and OST.
lain: A deeply peculiar piece — you could safely call it a strong cyberpunk classic — with a heavy, dense atmosphere.
severance: It explores the nooks of corporate culture rather nicely; it pushes against the boundaries a touch too subtly, but lands a finale that really did fire.
skins: I came to it late, which is a shame. The first two seasons stood on strong characterization — heavily exaggerated yet believable storylines and a broad sweep of subject matter.
solo-na-ugorze: Maybe a touch about nothing, but I'm terribly fond of the animation style — and the ending too, really.
sonny-boy: It's like a cross between Lord of the Flies, Antichamber and The Witness.
spec-ops: Fairly middling gameplay, paired with decent audiovisual presentation and a surprising gem at its very core — the story. Here for the narrative? Then run to the store! :)
squid-game: Otherworldly makeup and character design and, above all, original — executed with electrifying skill, packed with a vast array of socio-political-economic threads. A classic.
tekkonkinkreet: This film reaches heights few works of art ever do, and then climbs past them; it runs well overall, a coherent world — but it's the subtleties that truly floor you.
korra: An intimidatingly accomplished artistic layer, but the plot gallops along too fast; it lacks the long-game vision and the stronger suits the original had.
midnight-gospel: A series that feels made on hard drugs, yet a fair amount of detail has been infused into it, and the whole thing is, paradoxically, a very tasty watch.
tatami-galaxy: cosmic pace and intricacy, an intriguing concept
tori-no-uta: wow, what a structure — I find it rather fascinating how such a fairly amateur production can still stun you with the beauty of its visual and narrative style.
trigun: Great humor; it grates a little at times when it strays from its own mood, but on the whole it comes off splendidly.
utopia: The colors, the soundtrack and a creative, fresh approach lend this series a good deal of magic.
waltz-bashir: Maybe my memory is short, but I can't recall another animated war film — one that depicts conflict so naturalistically, in such a powerful, multi-vectored style.
watership-down: Gosh, what a marriage of elements.
wolfwalkers: Exquisite aesthetically, if not so much narratively. On one hand the aesthetic experience and the sheer artistry and craft are well worth appreciating, but I was left wanting a little more.
wonder-egg: Highly creative, superbly written, with a composition that toys with the viewer's perception in praiseworthy fashion — though certain elements come apart in the reception.
wolfs-rain: Very singular; unfortunately the dialogue can be tendentious and the middle drags terribly, but the soundtrack is sensational — thematically I know nothing else like it, and at times it blows you away.
cyberpunk-edge: A wildly uneven spectacle; it hits real highs and improves with time, yet it still feels like a recycled product, over-stuffed with details borrowed from elsewhere.
flcl: It allows itself some genuinely impressive creative feats — a tornado of the best and worst in anime, wrapped in a format rather more accessible than Kill la Kill.
steins-gate: I see a real feat here — the story holds together despite its intricacy. But it never truly leaps beyond that, which is a great shame.
terror-resonance: A great soundtrack, beautiful linework and palette; narratively odd and seemingly cliché, but in the end a great watch.
elfen-lied: A fantastic concept and a fiercely extreme series, yet a pervasive shallowness and immaturity bolted shut much of its genuinely vast potential.
linia-zycia: Hypnotic animation, artistic heights; perhaps it lacks a concrete punchline — but then, is one even needed?
siec: The experimentalism of this piece really sat well with me, the sonic experimentation especially — though I missed something more in it all.
three-colors-white: Overall weaker than Blue, but the narrative progression is excellent, with a superb, absolutely first-rate culmination.
imp-f-child: Artistically flawless, with fantastic camerawork — practically poetry that hypnotizes for the entire runtime.
imp-f-dekalog-v: Something remarkable happens here with the direction and cinematography; everything has its place, and a full cross-section of everything is held up to sharp scrutiny.
imp-f-dekalog-viii: Studded with references at a thoroughly world-class level, an atmosphere so thick you could bite into it; a very heavy yet natural episode in which everything has its place.
imp-g-608438: A genre classic, I'd say — part of my childhood :)
imp-f-zdj-cie: The archetype of journalism — how much they managed to draw out of a simple yet intriguing photograph, and to convey, makes a monumental impression and offers a singular insight.
imp-f-a-bronx-tale: There's something about this film whereby, even if the scale is theoretically smaller, even if it isn't fully a gangster picture, it's a very good watch and lands beautifully.
imp-f-a-king-in-new-york: I supposedly didn't care for parts of it, but I found plenty of timeless substance there, and its portrait of New York with all its intricacies won me over.
imp-f-aftersun: I bought into this level of sensitivity :) Even if there are smaller elements that didn't sit right with me, the film as a whole still seeps through you rather wonderfully.
imp-f-age-of-sail: A notch higher for the superb quality of the animation.
imp-g-606063: Remedy still on form. A sensational psychological thriller well worth playing — and at a sensational price, too!
imp-f-andrey-rublyov: Very, very, very good — mounted with sweep and that exquisite Tarkovsky direction that keeps your eyes riveted to the screen indefinitely.
imp-s-870863: There's something about these BBC nature productions — they weave their various elements together so cohesively and intriguingly that you really can take a great deal away from them.
imp-f-bab-el-hadid: Hm.. a fairly closed-off format; a few problems run through it, but it flashes with select performances, and flashes too with mood and narrative development.
imp-f-baraka: A rather fascinating journey around the world — beautiful shots and a kaleidoscope of a sliver of humanity.
imp-g-822028: For those who never played Half-Life but would like to, it's a superb trip back in time in fairly contemporary trappings. Surprising how well the old bones hold up.
imp-f-c-era-una-volta-il-west: Sensational atmosphere, a coherent plot — everything checks out and it's all good.
imp-f-cafarna-m: Heavy, grimy realism, captured in none-too-refined direction, but steeped in substance like few others.
imp-f-dekalog-iii: Finally something more creative — sensational, rather beautiful direction and play of light; the script is still a touch dry, but the execution takes a step forward in episode III.
imp-f-dekalog-iv: Its play with subtleties ignites the whole thing, stylistically and substantively, with a twist that's exhaustively explored and phenomenally adapts the context of the fourth commandment.
imp-f-dekalog-ix: The most complete episode — an incredible number of concepts packed in, decisive directorial heights; in substance, it's among the world's best.
imp-f-dekalog-vii: The conceptual twist is world-class; the balancing of human relationships and the narrative progression, combined with great direction, is at times downright staggering.
imp-f-die-nash-rner: A little incoherent, but in turn very substantial, the first half especially.
imp-f-dirty-harry: A great soundtrack, and a screenplay that builds out nicely and raises the bar.
imp-f-duet: Top-class animation that wants only a touch of a decidedly stronger script — it was already building and then suddenly broke off..
